CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 13)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 13)

Recap from last lecture

Local recoverable codes:

An [n,k]q[n, k]_q[n,k]q​ code is called rrr-locally recoverable if

  • every codeword symbol yjy_jyj​ has a recovering set Rj⊆[n]∖jR_j \subseteq [n] \setminus jRj​⊆[n]∖j ([n]={1,2,…,n}[n]=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}[n]={1,2,…,n}),
  • such that yjy_jyj​ is computable from yiy_iyi​ for all i∈Rji \in R_ji∈Rj​.
  • ∣Rj∣≤r|R_j| \leq r∣Rj​∣≤r for every j∈nj \in nj∈n.

Bounds for locally recoverable codes

Bound 1: kn≤rr+1\frac{k}{n}\leq \frac{r}{r+1}nk​≤r+1r​.

Bound 2: d≤n−k−⌈kr⌉+2d\leq n-k-\lceil\frac{k}{r}\rceil +2d≤n−k−⌈rk​⌉+2. (r=kr=kr=k gives singleton bound)

Turan’s Lemma

Let GGG be a graph with nnn vertices. Then there exists an induced directed acyclic subgraph (DAG) of GGG on at least n1+avg⁡i(diout)\frac{n}{1+\operatorname{avg}_i(d^{out}_i)}1+avgi​(diout​)n​ nodes, where dioutd^{out}_idiout​ is the out-degree of vertex iii.

Proof using probabilistic method.

Proof via the probabilistic method

Useful for showing the existence of a large acyclic subgraph, but not for finding it.

Tip

Show that E[X]≥something\mathbb{E}[X]\geq somethingE[X]≥something, and therefore there exists UπU_\piUπ​ with ∣Uπ∣≥something|U_\pi|\geq something∣Uπ​∣≥something, using pigeonhole principle.

For a permutation π\piπ of [n][n][n], define Uπ={π(i):i∈[n]}U_\pi = \{\pi(i): i \in [n]\}Uπ​={π(i):i∈[n]}.

Let i∈Uπi\in U_\pii∈Uπ​ if each of the dioutd_i^{out}diout​ outgoing edges from iii connect to a node jjj with π(j)>π(i)\pi(j)>\pi(i)π(j)>π(i).

In other words, we select a subset of nodes UπU_\piUπ​ such that each node in UπU_\piUπ​ has an outgoing edge to a node in UπU_\piUπ​ with a larger index. All edges going to right.

This graph is clearly acyclic.

Choose π\piπ at random and Let X=∣Uπ∣X=|U_\pi|X=∣Uπ​∣ be a random variable.

Let XiX_iXi​ be the indicator random variable for i∈Uπi\in U_\pii∈Uπ​.

So X=∑i=1nXiX=\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_iX=∑i=1n​Xi​.

Using linearity of expectation, we have

E[X]=∑i=1nE[Xi]E[X]=\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i]E[X]=i=1∑n​E[Xi​]

E[Xi]E[X_i]E[Xi​] is the probability that π\piπ places iii before any of its out-neighbors.

For each node, there are (diout+1)!(d_i^{out}+1)!(diout​+1)! ways to place the node and its out-neighbors.

For each node, there are diout!d_i^{out}!diout​! ways to place the out-neighbors.

So, E[Xi]=diout!(diout+1)!=1diout+1E[X_i]=\frac{d_i^{out}!}{(d_i^{out}+1)!}=\frac{1}{d_i^{out}+1}E[Xi​]=(diout​+1)!diout​!​=diout​+11​.

Since X=∑i=1nXiX=\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_iX=∑i=1n​Xi​, we have

Tip

Recall Arithmetic mean (1n∑i=1nxi\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_in1​∑i=1n​xi​) is greater than or equal to harmonic mean (n∑i=1n1xi\frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{x_i}}∑i=1n​xi​1​n​).

E[X]=∑i=1nE[Xi]=∑i=1n1diout+1=1n∑i=1n1diout+1≥n1n(1+∑i=1ndiout)=n1+avg⁡i(diout)\begin{aligned} E[X]&=\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i]\\ &=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^{out}+1}\\ &=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^{out}+1}\\ &\geq \frac{n}{\frac{1}{n}(1+\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{out})}\\ &=\frac{n}{1+\operatorname{avg}_i(d_i^{out})}\\ \end{aligned}E[X]​=i=1∑n​E[Xi​]=i=1∑n​diout​+11​=n1​i=1∑n​diout​+11​≥n1​(1+∑i=1n​diout​)n​=1+avgi​(diout​)n​​

Locally recoverable codes

Bound 1

kn≤rr+1\frac{k}{n}\leq \frac{r}{r+1}nk​≤r+1r​

Proof via Turan's Lemma

Let GGG be a directed graph such that i→ji\to ji→j is an edge if j∈Rij\in R_ij∈Ri​. (jjj required to repair iii)

diout≤rd_i^{out}\leq rdiout​≤r for every iii, so avg⁡i(diout)≤r\operatorname{avg}_i(d_i^{out})\leq ravgi​(diout​)≤r.

By Turan’s Lemma, there exists an induced directed acyclic subgraph (DAG) GUG_UGU​ on GGG with UUU nodes such that ∣U∣≥n1+avg⁡i(diout)≥n1+r|U|\geq \frac{n}{1+\operatorname{avg}_i(d_i^{out})}\geq \frac{n}{1+r}∣U∣≥1+avgi​(diout​)n​≥1+rn​.

Since GUG_UGU​ is a DAG, it is acyclic. So there exists a vertex u1∈Uu_1\in Uu1​∈U with no outgoing edges inside GUG_UGU​. (leaf node in GUG_UGU​)

Note that if we remove u1u_1u1​ from GUG_UGU​, the remaining graph is still a DAG.

Let GU1G_{U_1}GU1​​ be the induced graph on U1=U∖{u1}U_1=U\setminus \{u_1\}U1​=U∖{u1​}.

We can find a vertex u2∈U1u_2\in U_1u2​∈U1​ with no outgoing edges inside GU1G_{U_1}GU1​​.

We can continue this process until we have all the vertices in UUU removed.

So all symbols in UUU are redundant.

Note the number of redundant symbols =n−k≥n1+r=n-k\geq \frac{n}{1+r}=n−k≥1+rn​.

So k≤rnr+1k\leq \frac{rn}{r+1}k≤r+1rn​.

So kn≤rr+1\frac{k}{n}\leq \frac{r}{r+1}nk​≤r+1r​.

Bound 2

d≤n−k−⌈kr⌉+2d\leq n-k-\lceil\frac{k}{r}\rceil +2d≤n−k−⌈rk​⌉+2

Definition for code reduction

For C=[n,k]qC=[n,k]_qC=[n,k]q​ code, let I⊆[n]I\subseteq [n]I⊆[n] be a set of indices, e.g., I={1,3,5,6,7,8}I=\{1,3,5,6,7,8\}I={1,3,5,6,7,8}.

Let CIC_ICI​ be the code obtained by deleting the symbols that is not in III. (only keep the symbols with indices in III)

Then CIC_ICI​ is a code of length ∣I∣|I|∣I∣, CIC_ICI​ is generated by GI∈Fqk×∣I∣G_I\in \mathbb{F}^{k\times |I|}_qGI​∈Fqk×∣I∣​ (only keep the rows of GGG with indices in III).

∣CI∣=qrank⁡(GI)|C_I|=q^{\operatorname{rank}(G_I)}∣CI​∣=qrank(GI​).

Note GIG_IGI​ not necessarily of rank kkk.

Reduction lemma

Let C\mathcal{C}C be an [n,k]q[n, k]_q[n,k]q​ code. Then d=n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}d=n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}d=n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}.

Proof for reduction lemma

We proceed from two inequalities.

First d≤n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}d\leq n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}d≤n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}.

Let I⊆[n]I\subseteq [n]I⊆[n] with ∣CI∣≤qk|C_I|\leq q^k∣CI​∣≤qk.

Then there exists m1,m2∈Fqkm_1,m_2\in \mathbb{F}_q^km1​,m2​∈Fqk​ such that m1GI=m2GIm_1G_I=m_2G_Im1​GI​=m2​GI​.

Let y1=m1GIy_1=m_1G_Iy1​=m1​GI​ and y2=m2GIy_2=m_2G_Iy2​=m2​GI​.

Then y1,y2y_1,y_2y1​,y2​ have at least ∣I∣|I|∣I∣ entries in common.

So dH(y1,y2)≤n−∣I∣d_H(y_1,y_2)\leq n-|I|dH​(y1​,y2​)≤n−∣I∣.

So d≤n−∣I∣d\leq n-|I|d≤n−∣I∣ for every III such that ∣CI∣≤qk|C_I|\leq q^k∣CI​∣≤qk.

So d≤n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}d\leq n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}d≤n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}.

Now we show the other inequality.

d≥n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}d\geq n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}d≥n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}.

Let y1,y2∈Cy_1,y_2\in \mathcal{C}y1​,y2​∈C such that dH(y1,y2)=dd_H(y_1,y_2)=ddH​(y1​,y2​)=d.

Let JJJ be the set on which y1y_1y1​ and y2y_2y2​ identical.

So ∣J∣=n−d|J|=n-d∣J∣=n−d.

∣CJ∣<qk|C_J|<q^k∣CJ​∣<qk since y1y_1y1​ and y2y_2y2​ have at least ddd entries in common.

So max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}≥n−d\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}\geq n-dmax{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}≥n−d. (by existence of JJJ)

So d≥n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}d\geq n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}d≥n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}.

We prove bound 2 using the same subgraph from proof of bound 1.

Proof for bound 2

Let GGG be a directed graph such that i→ji\to ji→j is an edge if j∈Rij\in R_ij∈Ri​. (jjj required to repair iii)

diout≤rd_i^{out}\leq rdiout​≤r for every iii, so avg⁡i(diout)≤r\operatorname{avg}_i(d_i^{out})\leq ravgi​(diout​)≤r.

By Turan’s Lemma, there exists an induced directed acyclic subgraph (DAG) GUG_UGU​ on GGG with UUU nodes such that ∣U∣≥n1+avg⁡i(diout)≥n1+r|U|\geq \frac{n}{1+\operatorname{avg}_i(d_i^{out})}\geq \frac{n}{1+r}∣U∣≥1+avgi​(diout​)n​≥1+rn​.

Let U′⊂UU'\subset UU′⊂U with size ⌊k−1r⌋\lfloor\frac{k-1}{r}\rfloor⌊rk−1​⌋.

U′U'U′ exists since nr+1>kr\frac{n}{r+1}>\frac{k}{r}r+1n​>rk​ by bound 1, and kr>⌊k−1r⌋\frac{k}{r}>\lfloor\frac{k-1}{r}\rfloorrk​>⌊rk−1​⌋.

So GU′G_{U'}GU′​ is also acyclic.

Let N⊆[n]∖U′N\subseteq [n]\setminus U'N⊆[n]∖U′ be the set of neighbors of U′U'U′ in GUG_UGU​.

∣N∣≤r∣U′∣≤k−1|N|\leq r|U'|\leq k-1∣N∣≤r∣U′∣≤k−1.

Complete nnn to be of the size k−1k-1k−1 by adding elements not in U′U'U′.

∣CN∣≤qk−1|C_N|\leq q^{k-1}∣CN​∣≤qk−1

Also ∣N∪U′∣=k−1+⌊k−1r⌋|N\cup U'|=k-1+\lfloor\frac{k-1}{r}\rfloor∣N∪U′∣=k−1+⌊rk−1​⌋

Note that all nodes in GUG_UGU​ can be recovered from nodes in NNN.

So ∣CN∪U′∣=∣CN∣≤qk−1|C_{N\cup U'}|=|C_N|\leq q^{k-1}∣CN∪U′​∣=∣CN​∣≤qk−1.

Therefore, max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}≥∣N∪U′∣=k−1+⌊k−1r⌋\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}\geq |N\cup U'|=k-1+\lfloor\frac{k-1}{r}\rfloormax{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}≥∣N∪U′∣=k−1+⌊rk−1​⌋.

Using reduction lemma, we have d=n−max⁡{∣I∣:CI<qk,I⊆[n]}≤n−k−1−⌊k−1r⌋+2=n−k−⌈kr⌉+2d= n-\max\{|I|:C_I<q^k,I\subseteq [n]\}\leq n-k-1-\lfloor\frac{k-1}{r}\rfloor+2=n-k-\lceil\frac{k}{r}\rceil +2d=n−max{∣I∣:CI​<qk,I⊆[n]}≤n−k−1−⌊rk−1​⌋+2=n−k−⌈rk​⌉+2.

Construction of locally recoverable codes

Recall Reed-Solomon code:

{f(a1),f(a2),…,f(an)∣f(x)∈Fqk−1[x]}\{f(a_1),f(a_2),\ldots,f(a_n)|f(x)\in \mathbb{F}_q^{k-1}[x]\}{f(a1​),f(a2​),…,f(an​)∣f(x)∈Fqk−1​[x]}.

  • dim⁡=k\dim=kdim=k
  • d=n−k+1d=n-k+1d=n−k+1
  • Need n≥qn\geq qn≥q.
  • To encode a=a1,a2,…,ana=a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_na=a1​,a2​,…,an​, we need to evaluate fa(x)=∑i=0k−1fa(ai)xif_a(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}f_a(a_i)x^ifa​(x)=∑i=0k−1​fa​(ai​)xi.

Assume r+1∣nr+1|nr+1∣n and r∣kr|kr∣k.

Choose A={α1,α2,…,αn}A=\{\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\ldots,\alpha_n\}A={α1​,α2​,…,αn​}

Partition AAA into nr+1\frac{n}{r+1}r+1n​ subsets of size r+1r+1r+1.

Definition for good polynomial

A polynomial ggg over Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​ is called good if

  • deg⁡(g)=r+1\deg(g)=r+1deg(g)=r+1
  • ggg is constant for every AiA_iAi​., i.e., ∀x,y∈Ai,g(x)=g(y)\forall x,y\in A_i, g(x)=g(y)∀x,y∈Ai​,g(x)=g(y).

To encode a=(a0,a1,…,an)a=(a_0,a_1,\ldots,a_n)a=(a0​,a1​,…,an​), we consider a∈Fqr×(k/r)a\in \mathbb{F}_q^{r\times (k/r)}a∈Fqr×(k/r)​

Let fa(x)=∑i=0r−1fa,i(x)⋅xif_a(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{r-1} f_{a,i}(x)\cdot x^ifa​(x)=∑i=0r−1​fa,i​(x)⋅xi

fa,i(x)=∑j=0k/r−1ai,j⋅g(x)jf_{a,i}(x)=\sum_{j=0}^{k/r-1} a_{i,j}\cdot g(x)^jfa,i​(x)=∑j=0k/r−1​ai,j​⋅g(x)j, where ggg is a good polynomial.

Proof for valid codeword

If a≠ba\neq ba=b, then (fa(αi))i=1n≠(fb(αi))i=1n(f_a(\alpha_i))_{i=1}^{n}\neq (f_b(\alpha_i))_{i=1}^{n}(fa​(αi​))i=1n​=(fb​(αi​))i=1n​.

  • deg⁡fa=k−2\deg f_a = k-2degfa​=k−2
  • Maximum degree monomial in faf_afa​: ar−1,k/r−1⋅g(x)k/r−1⋅xr−1a_{r-1,k/r-1}\cdot g(x)^{k/r-1}\cdot x^{r-1}ar−1,k/r−1​⋅g(x)k/r−1⋅xr−1.
  • deg⁡fa≤kr−1+kr−1=k−2\deg f_a \leq \frac{k}{r}-1+\frac{k}{r}-1=k-2degfa​≤rk​−1+rk​−1=k−2.

By Bound 1: k−2≤n−2k-2\leq n-2k−2≤n−2.

If fa(αi)i=1n=fb(αi)i=1nf_a(\alpha_i)_{i=1}^{n}=f_b(\alpha_i)_{i=1}^{n}fa​(αi​)i=1n​=fb​(αi​)i=1n​. then

  • faf_afa​ and fbf_bfb​ agree on more points than their degree.
  • a=ba=ba=b, a contradiction.

Corollary: ∣C∣=qk|C|=q^k∣C∣=qk.

In other words,

C={(fa(αi))i=1n∣a∈Fqk}\mathcal{C}=\{(f_a(\alpha_i))_{i=1}^{n}|a\in \mathbb{F}_q^k\}C={(fa​(αi​))i=1n​∣a∈Fqk​}

For data a∈Fqka\in \mathbb{F}_q^ka∈Fqk​, the iiith server stores fa(αi)f_a(\alpha_i)fa​(αi​).

Note C\mathcal{C}C is a linear code. and dim⁡C=k\dim \mathcal{C}=kdimC=k.

Let α∈{α1,α2,…,αn}\alpha\in \{\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\ldots,\alpha_n\}α∈{α1​,α2​,…,αn​}.

Suppose α∈A1\alpha\in A_1α∈A1​.

Locality is ensured by recovering failed server α\alphaα by fa(αi)f_a(\alpha_i)fa​(αi​) from {fa(β):β∈A1∖{α}}\{f_a(\beta):\beta\in A_1\setminus \{\alpha\}\}{fa​(β):β∈A1​∖{α}}.

Note ggg is constant on A1A_1A1​.

Denote the constant by fi(A1)f_i(A_1)fi​(A1​).

Let δ1(x)=∑i=0r−1fa,i(A1)xi\delta_1(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{r-1} f_{a,i}(A_1)x^iδ1​(x)=∑i=0r−1​fa,i​(A1​)xi.

δ1(x)=fa(x)\delta_1(x)=f_a(x)δ1​(x)=fa​(x) for all x∈A1x\in A_1x∈A1​.

Constructing good polynomial

Let (Fq∗,⋅)(\mathbb{F}_q^*,\cdot)(Fq∗​,⋅) be the multiplicative group of Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​.

For a subgroup HHH of this group, a multiplicative coset is Ha={ha∣h∈H,a∈Fq∗}Ha=\{ha|h\in H,a\in \mathbb{F}_q^*\}Ha={ha∣h∈H,a∈Fq∗​}.

The family of all cosets of H:{Ha∣a∈Fq∗}H:\{Ha|a\in \mathbb{F}_q^*\}H:{Ha∣a∈Fq∗​}.

This is a partition of Fq∗\mathbb{F}_q^*Fq∗​.

Definition of annihilator

The annihilator of HHH is g(x)=∏h∈H(x−h)g(x)=\prod_{h\in H}(x-h)g(x)=∏h∈H​(x−h).

Note that g(h)=0g(h)=0g(h)=0 for all h∈Hh\in Hh∈H.

Annihilator is a good polynomial

Fix HHH, consider the partition {Ha∣a∈Fq∗}\{Ha|a\in \mathbb{F}_q^*\}{Ha∣a∈Fq∗​} and let g(x)=∏a∈Fq∗(x−a)g(x)=\prod_{a\in \mathbb{F}_q^*}(x-a)g(x)=∏a∈Fq∗​​(x−a). Then ggg is a good polynomial.

Proof

Need to show if x,y∈Hax,y\in Hax,y∈Ha, then g(x)=g(y)g(x)=g(y)g(x)=g(y).

Since x∈Hax\in Hax∈Ha, there exists h′,h′′∈Hh',h''\in Hh′,h′′∈H such that x=h′ax=h'ax=h′a and y=h′′ay=h''ay=h′′a.

So g(x)=g(h′a)=∏h∈H(h′a−h)=(h′)∣H∣∏h∈H(a−h/h′)g(x)=g(h'a)=\prod_{h\in H}(h'a-h)=(h')^{|H|}\prod_{h\in H}(a-h/h')g(x)=g(h′a)=∏h∈H​(h′a−h)=(h′)∣H∣∏h∈H​(a−h/h′).

By Fermat’s Little Theorem, (h′)∣H∣≡1(h')^{|H|}\equiv 1(h′)∣H∣≡1 for every h′∈Hh'\in Hh′∈H.

So {h/h′∣h∈H}=H\{h/h'|h\in H\}=H{h/h′∣h∈H}=H for every h′∈Hh'\in Hh′∈H.

So g(x)=(h′)∣H∣∏h∈H(a−h/h′)=∏h∈H(a−h/h′)=g(a)g(x)=(h')^{|H|}\prod_{h\in H}(a-h/h')=\prod_{h\in H}(a-h/h')=g(a)g(x)=(h′)∣H∣∏h∈H​(a−h/h′)=∏h∈H​(a−h/h′)=g(a).

Similarly, g(y)=g(h′′a)=∏h∈H(h′′a−h)=(h′′)∣H∣∏h∈H(a−h/h′′)=g(a)g(y)=g(h''a)=\prod_{h\in H}(h''a-h)=(h'')^{|H|}\prod_{h\in H}(a-h/h'')=g(a)g(y)=g(h′′a)=∏h∈H​(h′′a−h)=(h′′)∣H∣∏h∈H​(a−h/h′′)=g(a).
